food_name: Kopi-O (Traditional Singapore/Malaysia Kopitiam Black Coffee with Sugar)
region_of_origin: Hainanese immigrant coffee-shop (kopitiam) tradition in Singapore and Malaysia, established 19th–20th century
food_subgroup: Traditional kopitiam brewed black coffee served with sugar, without milk; 200ml cup; robusta-arabica blend roasted with sugar and butter (margarine)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kopi-o. Closest parent is brewed coffee (FOODON:03301103). Kopi-o is a culturally specific preparation not yet represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Coffee, brewed (closest parent match; no specific FoodEx2 term for kopi-o kopitiam preparation)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A044A is the closest defensible parent. No specific facet for kopitiam-style sugar-and-butter-roasted robusta blend exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for kopi-o or kopitiam coffee preparation.

main_ingredients: robusta-arabica coffee beans (roasted with sugar and margarine)

serving.description: 1 cup = 200ml (standard kopitiam serve; includes approximately 1.5 teaspoons added cane sugar unless ordered 'kosong' — without sugar)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 cup (200ml / approximately 200g), which is the standard kopitiam portion and includes approximately 1.5 teaspoons of added cane sugar unless the customer orders 'kopi-o kosong' (no sugar).

nutrient_source.title: Kopi O — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match to HPB Foodo entry for Kopi-O (black coffee with sugar, 200ml kopitiam serve). Values are per 100g/100ml as reported by HPB. Energy 25 kcal, protein 0.2g, fat 0.1g, carbohydrate 5.9g, sugar 5.7g per 100g. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore food composition data.

gi_source.title: Sucrose GI reference (GI=65) and cane sugar GI data; analogous estimate for kopi-o based on available carbohydrate from added cane sugar component
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kopi-o in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 32 based on the primary glycaemic contributor being added cane sugar (sucrose GI ~65) diluted in a predominantly water-based beverage. The effective GI of the beverage is substantially lower than sucrose alone due to the low carbohydrate density and coffee matrix effects (chlorogenic acids in coffee are documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response). Available carbohydrate per 200ml cup = approximately 11.8g (5.9g/100g × 200ml). GL = (32 × 11.8) / 100 = 3.8, rounded to 4.2 using the 13g sugar estimate inclusive of serving variation. This is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with published coffee-beverage GI literature.

hypertension.guidance: Kopi-O has very low sodium (5mg/100g, approximately 10mg per 200ml cup), posing negligible sodium burden for hypertensive patients; however, the high caffeine content (~100–120mg per cup from robusta-dominant blend) may transiently elevate blood pressure, and habitual consumption of 3+ cups per day with sugar (approximately 39g free sugar daily) is a secondary concern for blood pressure management in metabolic syndrome.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added cane sugar: approximately 11.8–13g per 200ml cup; GL approximately 4.2 per cup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Each 200ml cup of kopi-o with sugar contributes approximately 11.8–13g of free sugar and a glycaemic load of approximately 4.2; three cups per day adds approximately 35–39g of free sugar from beverages alone, substantially exceeding the WHO recommended limit of 25g free sugar per day — patients with Type 2 Diabetes should switch to kopi-o kosong (no sugar), which eliminates the carbohydrate burden entirely while preserving the potential insulin-sensitising benefits of coffee chlorogenic acids.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.0g/100g; negligible fat content overall
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kopi-O contains negligible fat (0.1g/100g total fat, 0.0g saturated fat), posing no direct lipid burden for cardiovascular disease patients; the primary cardiovascular consideration is the cumulative free sugar intake from habitual sweetened consumption, which contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and metabolic risk when consumed in excess of WHO free sugar guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kopi-O is the traditional black coffee of Singapore and Malaysia kopitiam (coffee shop) culture, prepared from a robusta-arabica blend of coffee beans that are roasted with sugar and butter or margarine in the Hainanese tradition, producing a uniquely caramelised, dark, and slightly oily flavour profile distinct from Western espresso or filter coffee. It is brewed by pouring boiling water through a cloth sock filter and served in a 200ml ceramic cup, sweetened with approximately 1.5 teaspoons of cane sugar but containing no milk. Per 200ml serving: approximately 50 kcal, 11.8–13g carbohydrate (entirely from added sugar), 0.2g protein, 0.1g fat, and caffeine approximately 100–120mg (robusta beans yield higher caffeine than arabica-only blends). GI approximately 32 (low), GL approximately 4.2 per cup. Kopi-O Kosong (without sugar) contains approximately 5 kcal and zero carbohydrate with the same caffeine content. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, switching to kosong eliminates the sugar burden while preserving the potential metabolic benefits of coffee. Three cups per day with sugar adds approximately 35–39g of free sugar, exceeding the WHO 25g/day free sugar limit. Source: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo). Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: kopi o vs kopi o kosong

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from an exact match in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented as such. All mandatory multilingual fields are complete.
notes: Kopi-O is a staple beverage at Singapore and Malaysian kopitiams and mamak stalls. The 'O' derives from Hokkien, meaning 'black' or 'original'. The traditional roasting process uses sugar and butter or margarine (not pure arabica beans), producing a flavour profile not equivalent to Western espresso. Kopi-O Ais is the iced variant. Kopi-O Kosong is the unsweetened variant (zero carbohydrate, same caffeine). The is_vegan flag is false because the roasting process traditionally uses butter or margarine (which may contain dairy). The is_dairy_free flag is false for the same reason. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are reported as -1.0 because these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this item; -1.0 is the Project Veritas convention for source-not-reported nutrients.
